Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a proper study routine. I'd mostly just watch the lecture videos when I felt like it, usually late at night when things were quiet. With 16 online courses, I couldn't really give each one dedicated time slots. I'd drift between subjects based on whatever felt least overwhelming in the moment, which wasn't the most efficient approach but it's how I managed.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching certain courses, then suddenly spend hours catching up when I realized deadlines were approaching. It wasn't intentional—life just got in the way, and honestly, my motivation to stick to a schedule was pretty low. I know I should have been more consistent, but I just couldn't bring myself to care enough to maintain that structure.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or realizing I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements I'd missed. It wasn't like I was excited to engage with the material—it was more about damage control and making sure I wasn't falling too far behind.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became something I naturally went to. I'd use it when I had to, but it always felt like a chore rather than a habit. Even when I tried to make it routine, the lack of internet at home made it difficult to access consistently, so I'd have to plan around when I could get online.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I'd play them while doing other things sometimes, just absorbing the information auditorily. The recorded sessions were good because I could pause and replay parts I didn't catch the first time. That flexibility worked well with how my brain processes information.
Q6: The discussion forums—I barely touched them. I posted maybe twice the whole semester because I didn't see the point in typing out my thoughts when I could just think them through myself. The reading materials I often skipped too, since listening to explanations worked better for me anyway. It felt like wasted effort to engage with formats that didn't match how I learn.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to the exam or assignments, it felt worth doing. Activities that seemed like busywork or padding just made me resentful of the time investment. I needed to feel like something would actually help my grade or understanding, otherwise my already low motivation made it easy to skip.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely ignorable to me. If something wasn't going to affect my grade, I couldn't justify spending time on it when I was already stretched thin across 16 courses. Required activities at least had consequences attached, which gave me enough external pressure to actually complete them even when I didn't feel like it.
Q9: Absolutely. Once I watched a video or downloaded materials, I'd study from my notes offline since I don't have reliable internet at home. I'd record myself summarizing concepts and listen back to those recordings. A lot of my actual learning happened away from any digital platform, just me with my handwritten notes and audio recordings.
Q10: Most of my exam preparation wouldn't show up at all. I'd listen to my own recorded summaries while commuting or doing chores, review handwritten notes for hours, and talk through concepts out loud to myself. The LMS would just show I logged in a few times, but the real studying was happening completely offline where no system could track it.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture videos when I had internet access, then watch them repeatedly offline over the following days. Same with PDFs and slides—I'd print them out or save them to my phone and study from those copies. One download could turn into hours of offline engagement that the system would never capture.
Q12: Around midterms, everything felt impossible. The stress was building up, and I just felt this heaviness that made even logging in feel exhausting. I was managing so many courses simultaneously, and my motivation was already low to begin with. There were days I'd stare at my laptop and just... not be able to make myself do anything productive.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or overwhelmed, the LMS became the last thing I wanted to deal with. I'd avoid it for days when competing responsibilities piled up. The medium stress level I constantly felt made it hard to engage meaningfully—I'd sometimes just click through things without really absorbing anything, just to feel like I'd done something.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through materials without actually reading them, just going through the motions. It was like my body was present but my brain had checked out. Those sessions felt hollow even though they'd register as activity.
Q15: The assignments were the worst for this. I'd know they were important but I'd put them off until the absolute last minute. I think it was a combination of low motivation and some kind of mental block—the bigger the task seemed, the more I'd avoid it. It's strange because I'd eventually do them and do well, but the avoidance was almost automatic.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without that external pressure, I'd drift indefinitely. I'd suddenly become very active in the LMS right before something was due, cramming all my engagement into short bursts. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could overcome my lack of internal motivation.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it gave me a brief boost—like validation that my last-minute efforts were paying off. But it didn't really change my behavior long-term. I'd feel good for a moment, then slip back into the same patterns. The feedback confirmed I could succeed without changing, which probably didn't help my motivation issues.
Q18: The course structure was flexible enough that I could get away with my erratic engagement patterns. Having recorded lectures meant I didn't have to be present at specific times, which suited me but also enabled my procrastination. If things had been more rigid, I might have been forced into better habits, though I probably would have resented it.
Q19: The asynchronous format made engagement easier in theory but harder in practice for someone like me with low motivation. Not having set times meant I could always tell myself I'd do it later. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't add much to my learning. Having more audio-based content would have suited my learning style better.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly, clicking around, watching bits of videos, but never actually producing anything. Sometimes you're trying to engage but your brain won't cooperate. Or maybe they're searching for something specific and getting lost. High activity doesn't mean productive activity.
Q21: That's basically my story. My exam scores and assignment grades were excellent despite barely showing up in the LMS. I did most of my actual learning offline through methods that worked for my auditory style. The system only saw the tip of the iceberg—the brief moments I was online—not the hours of offline study that led to my results.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people access things and how often, which might indicate who's struggling to even show up. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and all offline learning. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing while scrolling their phone. They measure presence, not learning.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually thinking about the material and making connections, regardless of where or how that happens. It's not about clicking buttons or posting in forums—it's about the mental work of understanding concepts. I can be deeply engaged while lying in bed listening to my own recorded notes, with zero digital footprint to show for it.
Q24: The LMS would never show my CBT sessions or how I was managing medium stress levels throughout the semester. It wouldn't capture my struggles with motivation or how much effort it took just to log in sometimes. It also wouldn't show the hours I spent studying through audio recordings and handwritten notes, which is where most of my actual learning happened.
